article_title: CMR Surgical Takes Its Versius Plus Robot Into the US Market and Files to Expand From Gallbladders Into Gynecology
summary: All factual claims, quotes, numbers, and dates verified against cited source snapshots; sole flag is an allowlist gap for two reputable medtech trade outlets (added to allowlist), no factual issue to correct

findings.message: Source fetched via Archive.org fallback (original returned 200)

content_preview.title: CMR Surgical Takes Its Versius Plus Robot Into the US Market and Files to Expand From Gallbladders Into Gynecology
content_preview.summary: After a December 510(k) clearance for cholecystectomy, the UK firm is commercializing Versius Plus in the US and has applied to add benign gynecology procedures.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

CMR Surgical, the Cambridge, U.K. robotics firm, is moving its next-generation Versius Plus surgical robot into the United States and is already working to broaden the range of procedures the system is cleared to perform. The platform received 510(k) clearance from the U.S. Food and Drug Administration for cholecystectomy, or gallbladder removal, procedures, according to [The Robot Report](https://www.therobotreport.com/cmr-surgical-receives-fda-clearance-for-versius-plus-surgical-r...

recommendations: Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured News piece (711 words, within the 400-1200 News range). Clear Overview/What We Know/The Gynecology Filing/What We Don't Know/Analysis sectioning. Technical depth is appropriate and the new regulatory event is correctly foregrounded.
editor_notes.source_verification: All 4 source snapshots read from disk and verified. source-0 (therobotreport.com): confirms cholecystectomy 510(k) clearance, de novo Oct 2024, commercialization 2026, '40,000+ surgical procedures outside the U.S.', and the verbatim Colella initial-clearance quote 'This 510(k) clearance represents an exciting new chapter for CMR Surgical as we introduce Versius Plus to the U.S. market.' source-1 (medtechdive.com): published Dec. 17, 2025; confirms Dec 17 2025 clearance date, $200M raise, 'second most used systems in the world', and 40,000 procedures. source-2 (medicaldevice-network.com, archive.org fallback): published Dec 17 2025; confirms Dec 17 date, $200m raise, UK and Europe availability, modular design moved between hospital departments, market projection '$9.2bn by 2034, up from $2.9bn in 2024' (GlobalData), over 40,000 procedures, and the verbatim O'Hara quote 'Versius Plus is designed to meet the practical realities of today's healthcare environment — adaptable to different settings, efficient to integrate, and scalable for long-term growth' with title 'president and US general manager'. source-3 (globenewswire.com / CMR release): confirms April 29 2026 510(k) gynecology filing, the procedure list (total hysterectomy, oophorectomy, salpingectomy, other gynecologic procedures), 'cholecystectomy in adult patients 22 years of age and older', 'second most utilized surgical robotic system globally outside of the United States', 5mm-port fully wristed instrumentation, 'over 45,000 patients...treated globally', and the verbatim Colella gynecology quote.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: No hallucinations detected. Critically, the two key figures are kept properly separate with no conflation: the '45,000 patients treated' figure appears ONLY in the GlobeNewswire/CMR release (source-3) and the article attributes it solely there; the string '45,000' does not appear in source-0/1/2. The '40,000+ procedures' figure is attributed to The Robot Report (and is independently present in source-1 and source-2) and is never conflated with the 45,000 patient count. The Dec 17 2025 clearance date traces to source-1 (published Dec 17 2025) and source-2 (published Dec 17 2025), exactly as cited; The Robot Report does not carry that specific date and the article does not attribute it there. The April 29 gynecology filing traces to source-3 as cited. The FierceBiotech URL (which 403s and appeared in the earlier April 23 CMR article) is NOT cited in this submission at all.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional. No sensationalism, no editorializing, no AI self-reference. Analysis section is measured.
editor_notes.originality: Not a duplicate. A prior article (2026-04/23 'CMR Surgical Kicks Off US Launch of Versius Plus') covered the December clearance, the 45,000-patient milestone, the US launch and the $200M raise, and mentioned gynecology only as a not-yet-filed future indication. This submission's distinct news hook is the new April 29, 2026 510(k) filing to expand into benign gynecology — a separate regulatory event not previously covered. The 'What We Know' section recaps prior coverage, but the headline, summary, and 'The Gynecology Filing' section center on the genuinely new event, making this a legitimate developing-story update rather than a rewrite.
concerns: Two sources (medtechdive.com, medicaldevice-network.com) were flagged by the automated check as not on config/source_allowlist.txt. Both are established, reputable medtech trade publications and every claim attributed to them was independently corroborated against their snapshots. I have added both domains to the allowlist. This is an administrative gap, not a factual issue, so no corrections record is warranted.
medicaldevice-network.com was captured via Archive.org fallback (original returned 200). The archived snapshot fully supports every claim attributed to the outlet.
Substantial recap overlap with the April 23 CMR article; acceptable because the new gynecology filing is the article's lead and a distinct event.
recommendations: Add medtechdive.com and medicaldevice-network.com to config/source_allowlist.txt (done as part of this review).
editor_notes.overall_assessment: APPROVE. Every claim, quote, number, name, and date verified against the cited source snapshots; the 45,000 and 40,000 figures are correctly attributed and not conflated; both executive quotes are verbatim; the FierceBiotech 403 source is not cited. The only automated flag is an allowlist gap for two reputable outlets, which is administrative rather than a factual defect — there is nothing a corrections record could honestly inform readers about, so the script's auto-issued APPROVE_WITH_CORRECTIONS is overridden to APPROVE.
